threats.title: Truncation in FRouter constant-product math allows draining pair reserves
threats.hypothesis_description: FRouter.getAmountsOut calculates the new reserve using integer division (k / newReserve). Because integer division truncates (rounds down), the calculated newReserve is smaller than the exact mathematical value. The output amount is calculated as (reserve - newReserve), meaning the rounding down of the reserve results in rounding UP the amountOut. The user receives more tokens than the constant product curve dictates. FPair.swap does not enforce the k-invariant (reserve0 * reserve1 >= k), so the actual product of the pool's reserves strictly decreases with each swap. An attacker can perform many small swaps optimized to maximize the fractional truncation, systematically draining the pool's assets.
threats.investigation_rationale: Custom AMM math is highly susceptible to rounding errors. The lack of a k-invariant check in the pair contract means rounding errors accumulate in the attacker's favor, breaking the fundamental economic security of the bonding curve.

proof_of_signal.pattern: newReserveA = k / newReserveB; amountOut = reserveA - newReserveA;

preconditions: Attacker can perform multiple swaps in a loop

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Integer division truncation increases amountOut artificially
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Repeated swaps extract more value than deposited, draining the pool reserves over time

economic_questions: What is the maximum rounding error per swap?
How many swaps are required to drain a typical pool?
Do the buy/sell taxes offset the rounding profit?
validation_requirements: Write a PoC to loop swaps and measure reserve depletion
Calculate gas costs vs extracted value to confirm profitability

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (just enough to cover gas and small swap amounts)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI if gas costs are low and taxes don't consume the spread
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single block execution via smart contract loop

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Destroys the bonding curve pool liquidity

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Calculate exact truncation bounds based on k and reserve sizes

threats.title: Unprotected spot price usage in BondingTax allows flash loan sandwich attacks
threats.hypothesis_description: BondingTax.swapForAsset uses router.getAmountsOut to determine the expected output for its tax swap, and calculates minOutput based on this spot price. This function is triggered automatically at the end of FRouter.buy and sell if the fee recipient is the tax manager. An attacker can flash loan assets, heavily skew the Uniswap pool to crash the price of the tax token, execute a tiny trade on FRouter to trigger the tax swap at the manipulated price, and then reverse their Uniswap trade for a massive profit, stealing the accumulated tax revenue.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using AMM spot prices for slippage protection is a known anti-pattern that enables atomic flash loan exploits, allowing attackers to extract accumulated protocol fees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256[] memory amountsOut = router.getAmountsOut(amount, path); uint256 expectedOutput = amountsOut[1];

preconditions: Tax manager has accumulated taxToken balance
Uniswap pair exists with sufficient liquidity for flash loan manipulation
FRouter feeTo is set to taxManager

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Flash loan can crash the spot price of taxToken before the swap is triggered
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker buys back assetToken at a discount after the protocol dumps taxToken at the manipulated price

economic_questions: How much taxToken is typically accumulated before a swap is triggered?
What is the liquidity depth of the target Uniswap pools?
validation_requirements: Simulate flash loan manipulation of the Uniswap pool
Verify that FRouter.buy/sell successfully triggers the swap during the manipulated state

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Large flash loan (e.g., 100k+ USDC) to move Uniswap pool
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Flash loan fee + swap gas costs
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 99% of the accumulated taxToken value
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI relative to gas/flash loan fees

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires flash loan availability for the assetToken
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When BondingTax contract has a large balance of taxToken

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All accumulated tax tokens in BondingTax

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check typical minSwapThreshold and maxSwapThreshold values

threats.title: Flawed slippage calculation in AgentTax.dcaSell causes swaps to fail or be exploited
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentTax.dcaSell calculates minOutput as (amountToSwap * (DENOM - slippage)) / DENOM. This applies the slippage percentage to the input amount (taxToken) rather than the expected output amount (assetToken). If taxToken is worth significantly less than assetToken, minOutput will be massively higher than the expected output, causing all tax swaps to revert and locking the funds. If taxToken is worth significantly more, minOutput will be near zero, providing no slippage protection and allowing MEV bots to extract the tax value.
threats.investigation_rationale: Slippage must be calculated relative to the expected output price, not the input amount. This mathematical error either breaks core functionality (DoS) or removes all economic protection.
preconditions: taxToken and assetToken have different market values or decimals

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If 1 taxToken = 1000 assetToken, minOutput is 0.99, allowing 99.9% slippage exploitation
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: MEV bots can sandwich the transaction to extract the difference between expected output and the artificially low minOutput

economic_questions: What are the typical price ratios between taxToken and assetToken?
Are the decimals of the two tokens different?
validation_requirements: Test dcaSell with tokens of different values to confirm revert or massive slippage exposure

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on pool size for sandwich attack
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Difference between actual value and artificial minOutput
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially massive if price ratio is highly skewed

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Requires active trading pool for the tokens
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of creator and protocol tax revenue

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All tax revenue processed via dcaSell

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare decimals and prices of configured taxToken and assetToken
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify the math error in minOutput calculation

threats.title: FERC20 burnFrom does not reduce totalSupply, inflating graduated agent tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: FERC20.burnFrom and _burn decrement user balances but fail to decrement _totalSupply. When a token graduates, Bonding._openTradingOnUniswap burns the pool's FERC20 tokens and uses the original _token.data.supply to mint the new agent token. Because the supply wasn't tracked correctly, the agent token is minted with an inflated total supply that doesn't match the circulating FERC20 tokens. This breaks the 1:1 unwrap mapping and dilutes the value of the graduated token.
threats.investigation_rationale: Breaking the ERC20 supply conservation invariant causes downstream accounting failures, specifically during the critical graduation phase where tokenomics are migrated to the AgentFactory.
preconditions: Tokens have been burned via burnFrom or _burn prior to graduation

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalSupply() remains constant while sum(balances) decreases
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Agent token is minted using stale _token.data.supply
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Creates phantom supply of agent tokens that are not backed by circulating FERC20 tokens, diluting real holders

economic_questions: Who receives the excess agent tokens minted during graduation?
Does the inflated supply break the Uniswap pool initialization?
validation_requirements: Trace the exact amounts passed to executeBondingCurveApplication
Verify if excess tokens are locked in the pair or accessible

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value dilution rather than direct extraction

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilutes token value and breaks 1:1 unwrap peg

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Market cap of the graduated token

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check how AgentFactoryV3 handles the totalSupply parameter

threats.title: Missing access control on Bonding.unwrapToken allows forced conversions
threats.hypothesis_description: Bonding.unwrapToken is a public function that takes an array of accounts, burns their FERC20 tokens, and transfers agent tokens to them. Because it lacks access control and Bonding is the owner of the FERC20 contract (allowing it to call burnFrom), anyone can force any user to convert their tokens. This can be used as a griefing attack to disrupt users' holding strategies or smart contracts that expect to hold FERC20.
threats.investigation_rationale: State-changing functions that modify other users' balances must have strict access control or require user consent (allowance/signatures).

proof_of_signal.pattern: function unwrapToken(address srcTokenAddress, address[] memory accounts) public {

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: token.burnFrom(acc, balance) called without checking msg.sender == acc
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker forces conversion of victim's portfolio
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Primarily a griefing vector, though could be combined with supply accounting bugs to force victims into a diluted asset

economic_questions: Are there tax implications or secondary market differences between FERC20 and the agent token?
Does forced unwrapping break smart contract integrations holding FERC20?
validation_requirements: Verify that an unprivileged caller can successfully execute unwrapToken for arbitrary addresses

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for iterating array and token transfers

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Disrupts user portfolios and contract accounting

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check if agentToken has transfer fees that would penalize the victim
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify burnFrom access control allows Bonding contract

threats.title: Per-transaction cap in Genesis allows whales to dominate allocations
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.participate enforces virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount. However, this check is per-transaction, not per-user. A user can bypass the intended maximum contribution limit by calling participate multiple times in a loop. This allows wealthy participants to dominate the genesis event and capture a disproportionate share of the agent tokens, defeating the purpose of the cap.
threats.investigation_rationale: Economic caps are designed to ensure fair distribution. If the cap is only per-transaction, it acts merely as a gas tax rather than a true economic limit.
preconditions: maxContributionVirtualAmount is set to a restrictive value

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Check is on virtualsAmt, not mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] + virtualsAmt
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Sybil/looping transactions to accumulate massive mapAddrToVirtuals balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Whale captures majority of agent tokens, centralizing supply and potentially manipulating secondary markets later

economic_questions: Is the cap intended to be per-user or just per-transaction?
Does dominating the genesis allocation grant governance control?
validation_requirements: Verify that a single address can accumulate more than maxContributionVirtualAmount via multiple calls

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Monopoly control over new agent token
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on agent token market performance

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Centralizes token supply, harming protocol decentralization goals

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review deployment scripts for maxContributionVirtualAmount intent

threats.title: Missing array length validation in Airdrop leads to stuck tokens or garbage transfers
threats.hypothesis_description: Airdrop.airdrop uses inline assembly to loop through _recipients and _amounts based solely on _amounts.length. It does not verify that _recipients.length == _amounts.length. If _recipients is shorter, the loop will read out-of-bounds calldata, potentially sending tokens to unintended addresses. Furthermore, it does not verify that the sum of _amounts equals _total. If _total is greater, the excess tokens are permanently stuck in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Assembly-level array iteration without length matching is a classic vulnerability that leads to memory/calldata corruption and loss of funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: let sz := _amounts.length ... let recp := calldataload(add(_recipients.offset, offset))

preconditions: Caller provides mismatched arrays or incorrect _total

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Mismatched arrays cause calldata out-of-bounds reads
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Loss of funds (stuck in contract) rather than direct profit extraction

economic_questions: Can an attacker intentionally craft calldata to redirect airdrops?
Is there any recovery mechanism for stuck tokens?
validation_requirements: Test airdrop with mismatched arrays to observe calldata read behavior
Test with _total > sum to confirm tokens are stuck

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Analyze assembly offsets to determine exactly what address is read when out of bounds

threats.title: Double nonReentrant modifier bricks Bonding.launch function
threats.hypothesis_description: Bonding.launch has the nonReentrant modifier and internally calls launchFor, which also has the nonReentrant modifier. Because OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard prevents nested calls to protected functions, calling launch will always revert. Users are forced to use launchFor, which requires them to manually specify the creator address, opening the door to attribution errors or spoofing.
threats.investigation_rationale: A core protocol function is permanently broken due to modifier misuse, forcing reliance on a secondary function that exposes user-controlled attribution parameters.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function launch(...) public nonReentrant { return launchFor(...); } function launchFor(...) public nonReentrant {

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Forces use of launchFor where creator is an arbitrary input
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can launch tokens attributing them to popular figures to spoof legitimacy, or users may accidentally attribute to wrong addresses losing tax revenue

economic_questions: Does spoofing the creator address provide economic benefits?
Are creator fees in AgentTax permanently lost if attributed to a contract that cannot claim them?
validation_requirements: Verify launchFor() accepts arbitrary creator addresses

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Failed transactions, potential loss of creator fees if spoofed

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check if UI handles the broken launch function

threats.title: Zero total supply edge case allows immediate execution of malicious proposals
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentDAO.state() returns ProposalState.Succeeded if forVotes == getPastTotalSupply(snapshot). If no tokens have been delegated yet, getPastTotalSupply is 0. A proposal with 0 forVotes will satisfy 0 == 0 and be considered Succeeded. If the proposal threshold is 0, or if the admin bypasses the threshold to create a proposal, an attacker can immediately call execute() on the proposal before anyone can vote against it, potentially draining DAO assets or altering critical parameters.
threats.investigation_rationale: Governance logic that auto-executes based on a percentage of total supply must strictly handle the zero-supply edge case to prevent vacuous truth exploitation.
deployment_assumptions: Proposal threshold is 0 OR admin creates the proposal
No tokens have been delegated prior to the proposal snapshot
threats.config_evidence: AgentDAO initialization takes threshold as a parameter. If deployed with 0, it is vulnerable.
preconditions: Total delegated supply at snapshot block is 0
Attacker can create a proposal (threshold is 0 or bypassed)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker submits malicious payload and immediately executes it
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker drains all assets held by the AgentDAO or its TBA

economic_questions: What assets does the AgentDAO control at initialization?
Is the proposal threshold strictly > 0 in production deployments?
validation_requirements: Deploy AgentDAO, ensure 0 delegation, create proposal, and attempt immediate execution

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All assets controlled by the DAO

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after DAO creation before delegations occur
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Complete compromise of the specific AgentDAO

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check deployment scripts for threshold values
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify OpenZeppelin Governor execute() behavior with state() override

threats.title: Multiple calls to onGenesisSuccess overwrite user claims
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.onGenesisSuccess assigns claimable tokens using '=' instead of '+='. Because the function lacks a terminal state flag (like isSucceeded), the FACTORY_ROLE can call it multiple times. A compromised or malicious operator could call it a second time with different arrays, overwriting the claimableAgentTokens of users who haven't claimed yet, effectively stealing their allocations.
threats.investigation_rationale: State transitions that distribute financial claims must be strictly one-shot or monotonically increasing to prevent rug pulls by privileged roles.
preconditions: FACTORY_ROLE is compromised or acts maliciously

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Assignment (=) instead of addition (+=) combined with lack of terminal state guard
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Operator calls function again with attacker-controlled addresses
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker steals unclaimed agent tokens allocated to legitimate users

economic_questions: How long do users typically take to claim tokens?
Is the FACTORY_ROLE secured by a multisig?
validation_requirements: Verify that onGenesisSuccess can be called a second time without reverting

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After success but before users claim

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Total loss of allocated agent tokens

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review FACTORY_ROLE assignment in deployment scripts

threats.title: withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized allows admin to steal unclaimed user tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized allows the DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE to withdraw any ERC20 token from the contract after finalization. This includes the agentTokenAddress. Since users must manually call claimAgentToken to receive their allocations, the admin can call this function to drain the contract of agent tokens before users claim them, leaving the contract insolvent and users unable to claim.
threats.investigation_rationale: Admin sweep functions must explicitly protect assets that are earmarked as liabilities (user claims) to maintain solvency invariants.

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin sweeps tokens backing user claims

economic_questions: Is the DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE secured by a multisig?
Is there any off-chain monitoring for this sweep function?
validation_requirements: Verify admin can withdraw agentTokenAddress while claims are pending

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Total loss of allocated agent tokens
